Page 23 (PDF page 23/28), Section 4 (Conclusion), first bullet in the list of open questions.

Is there a polynomial time algorithm to find \(\alpha\)-approximately JEF+PO lotteries in two-sided markets, for any constant \(\alpha\)?
title: Polynomial-time constant-factor approximation for justified-envy-free Pareto-optimal lotteries in two-sided markets
background_latex:
A two-sided (bipartite) cardinal-utility matching market consists of two agent sets \(A\) and \(B\) with \(|A|=|B|=n\). Each agent \(i\in A\) has nonnegative cardinal utilities \(u_{ij}\ge 0\) for matching with agent \(j\in B\), and each agent \(j\in B\) has nonnegative utilities \(w_{ji}\ge 0\) for matching with \(i\in A\). A (fractional) allocation is a matrix \(x=(x_{ij})_{i\in A,j\in B}\) satisfying the fractional perfect matching constraints
\[
\sum_{j\in B} x_{ij}=1\quad\forall i\in A,\qquad \sum_{i\in A} x_{ij}=1\quad\forall j\in B,\qquad x_{ij}\ge 0.
\]
By the Birkhoff--von Neumann theorem, such an \(x\) can be interpreted as a lottery over integral perfect matchings; hence fairness and efficiency are considered ex ante.

The (expected) utility of agent \(i\in A\) under allocation \(x\) is \(u_i\cdot x_i := \sum_{j\in B} u_{ij}x_{ij}\). Similarly, the utility of agent \(j\in B\) is \(w_j\cdot x_j := \sum_{i\in A} w_{ji}x_{ij}\).

An allocation \(x\) is Pareto-optimal (PO) if there is no other feasible allocation \(y\) such that \(u_i\cdot y_i\ge u_i\cdot x_i\) for all \(i\in A\) and \(w_j\cdot y_j\ge w_j\cdot x_j\) for all \(j\in B\), with at least one strict inequality.

Justified envy-freeness (JEF) is a relaxation of envy-freeness intended for two-sided markets. For \(i,i'\in A\), define the subset of \(B\) agents that (weakly) prefer \(i\) to \(i'\) by \(\{j\in B: w_{ji}\ge w_{ji'}\}\). Agent \(i\) has justified envy toward \(i'\) under allocation \(x\) if
\[
 u_i\cdot x_i \,<\, \sum_{j\in B\, :\, w_{ji}\ge w_{ji'}} u_{ij}x_{i'j}.
\]
An allocation is JEF if no agent on either side has justified envy toward another agent on the same side (the analogous condition is required for pairs \(j,j'\in B\) using \(u\) to define which \(i\in A\) prefer \(j\) to \(j'\)).

An \(\alpha\)-approximately JEF allocation (for \(\alpha\ge 1\)) is a constant-factor relaxation of JEF in which the right-hand side above is allowed to exceed the left-hand side by at most a factor \(\alpha\) (the paper asks this notion only qualitatively, without fixing a particular formal inequality).
problem_definition_latex: Design a polynomial-time algorithm which, given any two-sided (bipartite) cardinal-utility matching market \((A,B,u,w)\), computes an allocation \(x\) that is Pareto-optimal and satisfies an \(\alpha\)-approximate version of justified envy-freeness for some absolute constant \(\alpha\ge 1\). Equivalently: determine whether there exists a constant \(\alpha\) such that \(\alpha\)-approximately JEF and PO lotteries can be found in time \(\mathrm{poly}(n)\) for all such markets, and if so, give such an algorithm.

location_in_paper: Page 23 (PDF page 23/28), Section 4 (Conclusion), first bullet in the list of open questions.
open_problem_quote: Is there a polynomial time algorithm to find \(\alpha\)-approximately JEF+PO lotteries in two-sided markets, for any constant \(\alpha\)?
subject_classification: Computational social choice and matching markets
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The question asks for an algorithmic existence/constructive result across all instances rather than a universal inequality that could be refuted by a small counterexample. While one can search for hard instances against specific candidate algorithms, there is no clearly specified finite-dimensional conjecture to falsify by automated enumeration alone.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate mechanisms/algorithms can be represented as code and evaluated on generated instances using automated checks for feasibility, (approximate) JEF violations, and Pareto-optimality (or lack of dominated improvements via LP). However, Pareto-optimality verification and especially designing provably PO mechanisms with approximation guarantees requires theoretical proof beyond empirical fitness, so automated evolution would provide only partial guidance.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.
